summary: The Gemara continues the parah adumah discussion - the verse 'l'mishmerit' excludes Tur Shimon as a valid location, amoraim dispute whether non-priest slaughter of the parah is valid, and the ish iti's impurity mechanism and immersion requirement are clarified.

display.whats: Does a non-priest invalidate the parah slaughter, and what is the legal basis for the ish iti's impurity?
display.hint: The verse 'l'mishmerit l'mei nidda' does double duty: it establishes geographic requirements (excluding Tur Shimon) and implicitly addresses personnel requirements (a kohen for slaughter). The ish iti's impurity is rabbinic but immersion is still required.
display.title: Tur Shimon Excluded, and the Ish Iti's Rabbinic Impurity

learning.ahaMoment: The term 'l'mishmerit' (for keeping/guarding) is read as restricting where parah can be performed - a geographic halakha derived from a word about storage.
learning.memoryAnchor: The word 'mishmerit' restricts both location (not Tur Shimon) and handling (a kohen for slaughter) - one word, two limiting functions.
learning.learnerQuestion: What does the verse 'l'mishmerit l'mei nidda' exclude, and why must the ish iti immerse if his impurity is only rabbinic?
learning.coreTension: The verse 'l'mishmerit l'mei nidda' must do double duty - excluding Tur Shimon geographically while the ongoing non-priest slaughter dispute and the ish iti's impurity status (biblical or merely rabbinic) remain unresolved.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara reads 'l'mishmerit' as excluding Tur Shimon from valid parah locations, and resolves the ish iti dispute (R. Yehuda: no immersion needed since impurity is rabbinic; Sages: immerse anyway) by ruling that he immerses and waits until nightfall regardless.

takeaway.text: Tur Shimon is excluded as a valid location for the parah rite by the verse 'l'mishmerit l'mei nidda'; the ish iti's impurity is rabbinic, not biblical, but the final ruling requires him to immerse and wait until nightfall regardless.

learning.learningBlocker: Learners may assume the goat itself is impure like an ordinary impurity source, missing that its 'for Azazel' designation, not its physical state, is what causes the ish iti's impurity.

argumentFlow.speaker: Tanna
argumentFlow.text: 'L'mishmerit l'mei nidda' - the verse excludes Tur Shimon as invalid for the parah rite.
argumentFlow.label: Citation: 'L'mishmerit l'mei nidda' - the verse excludes

argumentFlow.speaker: Amoraim
argumentFlow.text: R. Ami: non-priest slaughter of parah valid. Rav: invalid.
argumentFlow.label: Dispute: R. Ami: non-priest slaughter of parah valid.

argumentFlow.speaker: R. Yehoshua bar Abba
argumentFlow.text: Objection: a baraita implies non-priest slaughter might be valid.
argumentFlow.label: Counter: Objection: a baraita implies non-priest slaughter might

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: From where do we include all parah stages in the law? Double expression 'statute of the Torah' teaches two things.
argumentFlow.label: Derivation: From where do we include all parah

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Elazar teaches: a kohen performs the parah rite; mixing is different and valid by anyone.
argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Elazar teaches: a kohen performs the parah

argumentFlow.speaker: Tanna/R. Yehuda
argumentFlow.text: R. Yehuda: ish iti need not immerse (rabbinic impurity). Sages: he must immerse.
argumentFlow.label: Dispute: R. Yehuda: ish iti need not immerse

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Final ruling: ish iti immerses and waits until nightfall.
argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Final ruling: ish iti immerses and waits

misconceptions.misconception: The ish iti becomes impure because the live goat itself carries ordinary ritual impurity, the way a corpse or other impurity-source would.
misconceptions.correction: The goat is alive and not impure in the ordinary sense; its designation 'for Azazel' gives it a special status that causes the one who carries it to become impure, a designation-based impurity rather than standard contact impurity.

visualizableElements.label: biblical vs rabbinic impurity of ish iti

quizSeeds.answer: Tur Shimon - a region outside the Land of Israel - as a valid location for the parah rite.
quizSeeds.question: What does 'l'mishmerit l'mei nidda' exclude?

quizSeeds.answer: The Sages enacted immersion even for rabbinic impurity to maintain the seriousness of the Yom Kippur rite.
quizSeeds.question: Why does the ish iti need to immerse if his impurity is only rabbinic?

sugyot.finalRuling: The ish iti must immerse and wait until nightfall; non-priest slaughter of parah is invalid according to the majority view.

glossary.en: A region east of the Jordan River, excluded as a valid location for the red heifer rite.
For keeping as waters of impurity (Num. 19:9) - a verse that restricts where the parah rite may be performed.

rashiTranslations.en: The verse 'it shall be kept for impurity water' (Num. 19:9) - excludes Tur Shimon from being a valid location for the parah rite.
Amoraic dispute: slaughter of parah by a non-priest - R. Ami says valid, Rav says invalid.
R. Yehoshua bar Abba raises an objection to the opinion that Rav supports from a different baraita.
From where is it derived to include all stages of the parah rite in the general law?
If the word 'law' includes all stages and 'statute' also includes all stages - what do we learn from 'statute of the Torah'?
The double expression 'statute of the Torah' teaches both that it is an eternal law and that it is a statute (chok) - without rational explanation.
Alternative: one term includes stages before; the other includes stages after.
The Gemara discusses what 'before' and 'after' mean in this context.
R. Yehoshua bar Abba's objection: the mixing stage is done by a non-priest and it is valid.
Counter: mixing is not a 'service' in the Temple sense; it can be done outside the Temple.
Rav's position refined: it is specifically slaughter by a non-priest that is invalid.
The verse specifying Elazar the priest is the source text - but Elazar himself was not the High Priest.
This teaches: the parah rite is performed by an ordinary kohen, not necessarily the High Priest.
Transition: the ish iti's impurity mechanism revisited.
The goat is alive when carried - how does a living creature convey impurity to its carrier?
Answer: the designation of the goat as 'for Azazel' gives it a special status that causes carrier impurity.
Parallel: the omer, the two loaves, and items consecrated for the altar can all affect purity status.
The impurity of the ish iti is a rabbinic ordinance, not biblical - but why was it enacted?
So that people should not say: the one who performed the Yom Kippur rite is the same as an ordinary person.
Alternatively: to prevent people from making the role of ish iti seem too light and unimportant.
The ish iti bathed, waited until nightfall, and then he was pure - this is the normal sequence.
R. Yehuda: the ish iti need not immerse - his impurity is only rabbinic and immersion was not required.
The Sages: even a rabbinically impure person should immerse before entering Jerusalem after Yom Kippur.
Tur Shimon is a region east of the Jordan - the verse excludes it as a valid location.
Why Tur Shimon specifically? It was outside the Land of Israel proper in rabbinic geography.
The exclusion: parah waters must be 'kept' inside the Land - Tur Shimon is outside.
Practical consequence: parah ashes from Tur Shimon are invalid for purification use.
The verse uses the word 'kept' (mishmerit) to teach permanence - the waters must be stored properly.
This also teaches: the waters cannot be prepared in an area that is questionable regarding purity.
Connection to the ish iti: the goat must be sent to a valid wilderness area, not just any place.
The cliff (Tsuk) is 12 mil from Jerusalem - this is well within the required zone.
The ish iti's journey takes him outside Jerusalem, where he completes his designated role.
After the goat is pushed off, the ish iti begins his return and his purification process.
He immerses at one of the ten booths on the way back.
The booths were established as rest stations and immersion sites for the ish iti.
The Gemara confirms: ten booths, ten stations, and the ish iti is provided for at each.
Final ruling: the ish iti must immerse and wait until nightfall regardless of the dispute.
The daf concludes the ish iti material and prepares to enter the incense offering discussion.
Transition: next Mishna deals with the incense - a crucial and secret Temple procedure.
The incense composition was known only to the Beth Avtinas family.
Review of 42b themes: verse analysis (Tur Shimon), parah slaughter, ish iti impurity mechanism.
Key insight: designation for a holy purpose can cause the designated item to affect carrier purity.
This principle will recur throughout the laws of terumah and Temple service.
The Gemara's legal reasoning here is tight: verse exclusions, inclusions, and purpose-based rules.
Halakhic summary: parah slaughter valid only by a kohen (not non-priest); ish iti is rabbinically impure.
The parah rite continues to be a paradigm case for Temple-specific rules that defy general principles.
Tur Shimon exclusion: regional geography matters for determining validity of ritual acts.
The verse 'for impurity water' is both descriptive and restrictive - it limits where the waters are valid.
R. Ami's position (non-priest slaughter valid) is a minority view; most hold Elazar implies a priest.
The debate continues: what exactly does 'Elazar' teach - only one-time, or all generations?
Answer: 'Elazar' establishes the paradigm; subsequent parah rites follow the same pattern.
Discussion of the parah rite in detail: burning, mixing, storing - each stage has its own rules.
The mixing of the ashes with water is the simplest act and can be done by anyone.
Sprinkling requires a pure person; storing requires proper vessels; all stages have verse support.
The Gemara completes its analysis of the parah service and its personnel requirements.
Summary: the parah rite requires a kohen for slaughter; other stages are more flexible.
The ish iti's role is complete once the goat has gone over the cliff.
His impurity lasts until nightfall; he immerses at a booth provided for this purpose.
The Yom Kippur avodah continues: the High Priest now prepares for the incense service.
42b ends with the resolution of the ish iti question and transition to the incense Mishna.
